content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance Administration (916) 322-5660 Executive Legal 322.5901 Enforcement 322.6441 Conflict of Interest 322.6444

June 9, 1978

Mr. Jack Allen
Acting City Attorney
450 North Crescent Drive
Beverly Hills, California 90210

Dear Mr. Allen:

Thank you for your letter of May 10, 1978, on behalf of
Councilmember Richard A. Stone, concerning the disqualification
provisions of the Political Reform Act. We hope that the
following advice may be helpful.

The facts as we understand them are as follows. Councilmember Stone, an attorney, represented the partnership known as
Maple Properties until that relationship was severed in January
of 1978. During the course of his representation, Mr. Stone
gave advice to Maple Properties concerning the acquisition,
financing, development, and other legal aspects of obtaining
real property owned by Litton Industries in the industrial area
of Beverly Hills. When it became apparent that representing
Maple Properties might interfere with his duties as a public
official, Councilmember Stone refrained from participating or
voting on any matters affecting the industrial area.

In January 1978, Councilmember Stone severed his relationship with Maple Properties. Although he has received income in
excess of $250 from Maple Properties for services rendered
through December of 1977, he is not now receiving any income
from Maple Properties, nor does he have any other financial
interest in the city's industrial area.

The industrial area is approximately ten blocks (40 acres)
in size. For many years, the area has been zoned for certain
industrial, commercial, and any residential uses. Currently,
the area contains industrial and commercial uses as well as
City of Beverly Hills public utilities and municipal service
facilities. The city is a large landowner in the area, owning

Mr. Jack Allen
June 9, 1978
Page Two


four large lots (6 acres), none of which are contiguous. Maple
Properties has acquired eight lots (8.5 acres), approximately
the same size as the city lots, comprising approximately two
square blocks in the industrial area. Three of the properties
owned by Maple Properties are separated either by city lot or
by a street. It would be a substantial advantage to Maple
Properties to have their properties consolidated, and it would
be a substantial advantage to the city if it could consolidate
its properties which are also divided by Maple's lots. There
is currently a moratorium on new building in the industrial
area. This moratorium is expected to expire at the end of
the year.

The planning commission has now proposed zoning for the
industrial area which would provide that two blocks on the
northeastern side of the industrial area be zoned for limited
commercial (LC) and that the remainder of the industrial area
would be zoned for multiple-family residential-planned development (RM-PD). Approximately one-third of the RM-PD zone is
owned by the Maple Properties, and it is the only owner in the
area that could possibly take advantage of the planned development provisions.

There are presently several matters before the Council
which involve the industrial zone. In June of 1978, the Council
will hold public hearings on an ordinance which establishes
the RM-PD zone, the LC zone, and also provides that developments
constructed for the purposes of providing houses for the elderly
and the handicapped shall have a maximum density of one hundred
units per acre and sets forth standards for such housing. The
RM-PD zone permits housing for the elderly and the handicapped,
but the provisions of this ordinance concerning housing for
elderly and handicapped persons have city-wide application.

Later this month, the Council will also consider an ordinance
establishing regulations for non-conforming uses throughout the
city. The ordinance provides that non-conforming uses be barred
within 20 years or if the property is not used for any purpose
for a six-month period. This ordinance will have particular
impact on the industrial area because, if the RM-PD zoning is
adopted, a number of current uses in that area will become nonconforming.

In addition, the city plans to develop a park in the industrial area. One option would provide that the city exchange
properties with Maple Properties so that Maple Properties would
have a complete two block area to build a planned development

Mr. Jack Allen
June 9, 1978
Page Three


as one project. The city would then take the properties which
it received from Maple Properties, condemn additional properties,
and consolidate all the property into a six acre park over an
underground municipal service center and parking facility. The
park would be immediately adjacent to the Maple Properties planned
development and would contain a baseball field and a soccer field.
Another option the Council may consider for the park in the
industrial area is the acquisition or condemnation of part or
all of Maple Properties' land holdings in the area.

     The question we have been asked to consider is whether
Councilman Stone must refrain from voting or participating in
these matters because of his former relationship with Maple
Properties. The Political Reform Act provides in Government
Code Section 87100:

     No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or
in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.

     Section 87103(c) further provides:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reason-
ably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:

          ...

     (c) Any source of income, other than loans by
a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is
made;...
                                   [Emphasis added.]

     Based on the facts and standards set forth above, Maple
Properties will be a source of income to Councilmember Stone
until January 1979. Therefore, Councilmember Stone is prohib-
ited from participating in any governmental decision between
now and next January in which it is reasonably foreseeable that
his source of income, Maple Properties, will be materially
affected.

Mr. Jack Allen
June 9, 1978
Page Four


The "twelve-month requirement" is designed in part to prevent
the situation in which an official severs his economic relation-
ship with his source of income immediately prior to participating
in the decision and then reestablishes the relationship after
the decision is made. Moreover, even if the relationship is
not reestablished, the previous income may have been provided
with a view to influencing the official. By providing that
an official may not participate in a decision which foresee-
ably and materially affects his source of income within twelve
months from the date of the last income transaction, the Act
insulates governmental decision-making from these potential
conflicts of interest.

With these general guidelines as background, we turn to
an analysis of several specific transactions. First, with
respect to the creation of the park which may involve a "land-
swap" or acquisition of Maple Properties' land holdings by the
city, Councilmember Stone is disqualified. From the facts set
forth in your letter, it appears that the land exchange would
permit Maple Properties to consolidate its holdings in the
industrial area, thereby facilitating development. We think
that such consolidation would materially benefit Maple Proper-
ties.

In addition, a decision to acquire the necessary land for
the park by eminent domain could seriously affect, if not
destroy, Maple Properties plan for development in the area.
Therefore, Councilmember Stone may not participate in any matter
involving the creation of a park in the industrial area.

Second, Councilmember Stone may participate in the deci-
sion of whether to adopt the ordinances establishing regulations
governing non-conforming uses. It is our understanding that
the ordinances in question would provide twenty years for Maple
Properties to abate any current non-conforming uses. In that
twenty year time period, a myriad of events could occur which
would prevent the non-conformity ordinances from materially
affecting Maple Properties. For example, Maple Properties
might sell its holdings or the city might rezone the area in
a manner which makes the holdings conforming. Moreover, if
Maple Properties were to construct condominiums according to
its present plans, the project would be in conformity with
the present zoning changes and, therefore, not subject to
the abatement ordinance.

Because the time frame is so long and the potential effects
so uncertain, we think it is not foreseeable that the ordinances
regulating non-conforming uses will materially affect Maple

Mr. Jack Allen
June 9, 1978
Page Five


Properties. Accordingly, Councilmember Stone need not disqualify
himself from participating on such matters.

     Lastly, you asked whether Councilmember Stone may partici-
pate in a decision to lift the current building moratorium and
rezone the area for multiple residences and limited commercial
use. From the facts set forth in your letter, we are unable to
determine whether such a decision would foreseeably and mater-
ially affect Maple Properties. You may wish to consider the
following factors in analyzing foreseeability and materiality
for yourself. It is our understanding that Maple Properties
could build condominiums under both the current and new zoning
ordinances. However, these two ordinances differ with respect
to the size and density of condominium units which can be con-
structed. If this difference has a material impact on Maple
Properties, Councilmember Stone would be prohibited from parti-
cipating in the consideration of the zoning change. Additionally,
the changes in permitted uses in the industrial area which result
from the zoning change could increase property values in that
area. If the zoning change does materially increase the value
of Maple Properties' land, then Councilmember Stone could not
participate in the zoning decision.

     It is our understanding that a decision to change the
current zoning also would have the effect of lifting the build-
ing moratorium presently applicable to the industrial area.
If the moratorium is not lifted, it will automatically expire
in January of 1979. If Maple Properties would be materially
affected by the early lifting of the moratorium, Councilmember
Stone should be disqualified.

     If we may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact our office.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight Dickerson
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DD:TP:LR:ma

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY

RICHARD W. MARSTON
CITY ATTORNEY

JACK ALLEN
SR ASST CITY ATTORNEY

MITCHELL H. KAHN
ASS'T CITY ATTORNEY

450 NORTH CRESCENT DRIVE
BEVERLY HILLS, CA 90210

AREA CODE 213
TELEPHONE 550-4877

CITY OF BEVERLY HILLS

May 10, 1978

Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Fair Political Practices Commission
110 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95184

Re: Possible Conflict of Interest: Councilmember Richard A. Stone

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

Our office has been requested by Councilmember Stone to request an
opinion from your office whether or not Councilmember Stone would
have a conflict of interest if he votes or participates in the
decision-making on matters affecting the Industrial Area within
the City of Beverly Hills.

The facts that are known to this office are that an existing
client of Councilmember Stone formed a partnership, Maple Properties,
which acquired properties belonging to Litton Industries in the
Industrial Area of this City. Mr. Stone also rendered advice to
Maple Properties concerning the acquisition. In early April 1977,
the client requested that Mr. Stone represent it on an ongoing
basis with respect to financing, development and other legal
aspects in relation to the real properties. Since it was apparent
in April of 1977 that Maple Properties would be negotiating with
the City for a possible exchange of properties, and that other
actions of the City, such as zoning, might materially affect the
client, Councilmember Stone, then Mayor of the City, disclosed
to the public and to the City Council that he was representing
Maple Properties and announced he would refrain from participating
or voting on any matter affecting the Industrial Area.

In early January 1978, Councilmember Stone, finding that his
representation in Maple Properties severely diminished his ability
as an elected member of the City Council to effectively represent
his constituents in major decisions involving the Industrial
Area, terminated his relationship with Maple Properties and its
principals and no longer retains Maple Properties or any of th

Mr. Dwight Dicker
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

May 10, 1978
Page 3

alternative locations and criteria be explored for possible re-
location of public service facilities out of the Industrial Area
or consolidation within the area of the existing public service
facilities located in the Industrial Area. It also provided that
the City should explore methods whereby a recreation area could
be assembled within the Planned Development Area.

Because studies for the future development of the Industrial Area
had not been completed and thus the revisions to the Land Use
Element and the implementing zoning regulations were not complete,
the City commenced processing an ordinance in July of 1976 which
would prohibit any construction in the Industrial Area for a
period of one year. The moratorium ordinance was adopted by the
City Council and became effective on January 20, 1977. Council-
member Stone voted for approval on the first reading of the
ordinance but did not vote on the second reading. Subsequently,
the moratorium was extended for another year in December of
1977, with Councilmember Stone not participating or voting.

At present, a plan has evolved which would provide that the City
would exchange properties with Maple Properties so that Maple
Properties will have a complete two-block area to build a planned
development as one project. The City would take the properties
which it received from Maple Properties, condemn additional
properties, and consolidate all the property into a six-acre
park over an underground municipal service center and parking
facilities. The park would be immediately adjacent to the Maple
Properties planned development. The park would primarily consist
of a Little League field and a soccer field, which are needed in
the community to satisfy Little League and American Youth Soccer
League requirements.

In addition, the Planning Commission has now proposed zoning for
the Industrial Area which would provide that two blocks on the
northeastern side of the Industrial Area be zoned for Limited
Commercial (LC) and that the remainder of the Industrial Area
would be zoned for Multiple-Family Residential -- Planned Develop-
ment (RM-PD). The RM-PD zone permits that properties in the area
may be developed to 25 units per acre and a maximum height of
45 feet with a maximum of 70% of the lot coverage or, alter-
natively, an owner of 2 1/2 acres or more may request a permit
for planned development in which case the developer will be permitted
to construct the buildings at the limit of 25 units per acre
but may construct the building to 75 feet in height providing
that the buildings only occupy 30% of the site. Owners of
the property would also be permitted to include convenience
retail shops in the planned development buildings to serve the
needs of the tenants of the area. The zone also provides for
municipal service facilities, municipal parks, housing for the
elderly and handicapped and other community related activities.

-3-

Mr. Dwight Dickerson                                          May 10, 1978
Fair Political Practices Commission                                    Page 2
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

principals as clients.  He received income in excess of $250.00
for services rendered for Maple Properties up and through December
1977.  He is not now receiving any income nor will he receive any
income, nor does he have any other financial interest in the
Industrial Area.

BACKGROUND

The Industrial Area consists of an area of the City approximately
two blocks (40 acres) in size. It is presently, and has been for
many years, zoned for certain industrial, commercial, and any
residential use.  The area consists of industrial and commercial
uses as well as public utilities and municipal service facilities
of the City of Beverly Hills.  The City is a large landowner in
the area, owning four large lots (6 acres), none of which are
contiguous.  Maple Properties has acquired eight lots (8.5 acres),
approximately the same size as the City lots, comprising approximately two square blocks in the Industrial Area.  Three of the
properties owned by Maple Properties are separated either by
City lots or by a street.  It would be a substantial advantage
to Maple Properties to have their properties consolidated, and
it would be a substantial advantage to the City if it could
consolidate its properties which are also divided by Maple
Properties.

Since 1965, the City's General Plan has called for elimination of
all the industrial uses in the Industrial Area and the creation
of a substantial park area in the Industrial Area.  In 1965, the
plan called for the entire Industrial Area to become public
property, but because of economics it never occurred.  In 1973,
the entire General Plan was under study for a revision, and
the Citizens Advisory Committees recommended again that the
industrial uses be eliminated from the Industrial Area and that
a park of approximately eight acres be established with the
remainder of the Industrial Area being re-zoned for multi-family
residential uses.

On May 17, 1977, after three years of study by the Planning
Commission and the Council, the Council adopted a revised Land
Use Element which provided that the Industrial Area became
planned development area with a permitted density of 50 units
per acre, a floor area ratio of 2.0:1 and a maximum height of
60 feet of any building.  The Land Use Element also provided
for parks to be located in the Industrial Area.  The Revised
Land Use Element was adopted by a 3 to 0 vote of the Council
with Mayor Stone not participating.

The revised Land Use Element required that new sections of the
zoning code be prepared to reflect the proposal for the Planned
Development Area in the Industrial Area and to prepare a Specific
Plan for the Planned Development Area.  The Element provided that

-2-

Mr. Dwight Dickerson                                          May 10, 1978
Fair Political Practices Commission                                   Page 4
Re:  Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone


Approximately one third of the RM-PD zone is owned by the Maple
Properties, and it is the only owner in the area that could
possibly take advantage of the planned development provisions.

POSSIBLE DECISIONS INVOLVING THE CITY COUNCIL

The following are potential decisions which the City Council will
have to make involving the Industrial Area and which Councilmember
Stone would or would not participate in depending upon whether he
has a conflict of interest.

On May 16th, the City Council will hold hearings to amend the
Land Use Element of the General Plan which reduces the densities
permitted in the Planned Development area from 50 units to 25
units an acre, but establishes the height limits as described
herein.  The amendment also provides for the Limited Commercial
area in the northeastern area of the Industrial Area.  Maple
Properties owns no interest in the Limited Commercial Area.  The
revisions also provide for the expanded open space areas for
Multiple-Family Residential Development.

On June 6, 1978, the Council will hold public hearings on an
ordinance which establishes the RM-PD zone, the LC zone, and
also provides that developments constructed for the purpose of
providing housing for the elderly and the handicapped shall be
permitted to build up to 100 units per acre and sets forth stand-
ards for such housing.  The RM-PD zone provides for housing for
the elderly and the handicapped, but the provisions of this
ordinance concerning housing for elderly and handicapped persons
have city-wide application.

On June 6, the Council will also consider an ordinance establish-
ing regulations for non-conforming uses throughout the City.  How-
ever, it will have particular impact on the RM-PD zone because if
the RM-PD zone is adopted, all private uses of property will be
non-conforming and would have to be abated at some time after
20 years.  It would affect Maple Properties only to the extent that
if Maple Properties chose not to develop their properties as multiple
family residential but to maintain them in their current status,
these uses would have to be abated after 20 years unless a use is
discontinued on any property for a period of six months, in which
case the use would have to be abated immediately.

The Council is strongly considering at present expanding the
park from six acres to a much larger park.  It is considering
possible acquisitions of property to the west of the existing City
properties which it may decide on June 6.  The Council is also
considering the possible acquisition or condemnation of part
of all of the Maple Properties.  These decisions will involve
the hiring of appraisers, the possible approval of the purchases
of the properties or the possible approval of condemnation actions
involving the acquisition of these properties.

                                -4-

Mr. Dwight Dickerson
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

May 10, 1978
Page 5

The Council will have to decide this summer whether to negotiate
the property exchange with Maple Properties and whether to
acquire other properties by acquisition or condemnation which are
presently planned for the park.

THE OPINION OF THIS OFFICE

It is the considered opinion of this office that if Councilmember
Stone participates in any of the decisions facing the Council
there would be no conflict of interest. The opinion of this
office is predicated on the basis that Councilmember Stone does
not own any property, has no investments in the Industrial
Area, and he has no client which has an interest in the Industrial
Area from which he is receiving income. Thus, Councilmember
Stone would not have an "economic incentive for defeating a
particular official matter without regard to its merits, or with
regard to its effect upon the official's pocketbook". (County
of Nevada v. MacMillen, 11 Cal.3d 662, 674 [1974]).

It has been said that the Political Reform Act of 1974 enforces
the concept that a man may not serve two masters- particularly
if one of the masters happens to be economic self-interest.
(See U.S. v. Mississippi Valley Company, 364 U.S. 520 [1960]).
When Councilmember Stone would no longer be subjected to a
second master, a master of economic self-interest.

We are aware of the provisions of Government Code Section 87100
which states:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in the making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a government decision which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

There would be no problem because it would be obvious since
on the facts stated the Councilmember has no present or future
interest in any decision involving the Industrial Area or the
Maple Properties which would benefit him. The problem is, however, with the definition of "financial interest" in Government
Code Section 87103(c). The applicable part of the Section
reads:

"87103. Financial Interest. An official has a
financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on:

. . . . .

-5-

Mr. Dwight Dickerson                                          May 16, 1978
Fair Political Practices Commission                                    Page 6
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone


     "(c) Any source of income, other than loans by
a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received
by or promised to the public official within
twelve months prior to the time when the deci-
sion is made; or . . . ."

This can be read two ways. The logical reading is that unless
any decision made by Councilmember Stone will have a material
financial effect on income he has received during the twelve
months prior to making the decision, he has no conflict of
interest. From the facts stated no such financial effect
will occur.

The other interpretation, one that does not make sense, is
that if Councilmember Stone has received income from Maple
Properties in the last twelve months prior to the decision
he cannot vote if the decision will have a material financial
effect on his former client. To read the Section that if a
councilmember, or for that matter, any affected official, is
disqualified from voting because he received income within
twelve months prior to making the decision, would appear con-
trary to the purposes of the Act and such an interpretation
could render the Section constitutionally vague and ambiguous.

There are too many situations which arise in which such an
application would not be rational. A few examples are set
forth herein:

     1. Councilmember Abel was a shoe salesman for Saks Fifth
Avenue during 1977 and received an income in excess of $250.
Councilmember Abel quit his job at Saks Fifth Avenue and went
to work as an automobile salesman for Beverly Hills Cadillac.
Saks Fifth Avenue applies for a building permit to double the
size of their store and the permit is denied by the Building
Director. Saks Fifth Avenue appeals the decision to the City
Council. Must Councilmember Abel, who will not be financially
affected in any way by the decision, but his decision will
have a material financial effect on Saks Fifth Avenue, be
prohibited from voting on the application?

     2. Councilmember Baker is an independent contractor who
sells encyclopedias. He sells a set of encyclopedia to
Mr. Charlie from which he deducts a commission of $300 during
1977. Later, Mr. Charlie applies to the City Council for a
permit to operate a private club. Is Councilmember Baker
precluded from voting on Mr. Charlie's application for a
private club merely because he received income in excess of
$250 from Mr. Charlie in the previous 12 months?


                              -6-

Mr. Dwight Dickers
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

May 10, 1978
Page 7

3. Dr. Dentist is a Councilmember who provides dental ser-
vices for the Flintstone family and has been paid more than $500
in dental fees by Mr. Flintstone during the last twelve months.
Mr. Flintstone asks the City Council to relieve him of the
requirement that he be required to provide 50 parking spaces on
his building site and that he be permitted to provide close to 50
spaces within 500 feet of his building. When Dr. Dentist learns
that Mr. Flintstone is going to make such an application to the
City, he tells Mr. Flintstone that he can no longer be the
Flintstone family dentist. Is Dr. Dentist precluded from voting
as a Councilmember on Mr. Flintstone's application because he
received income from Mr. Flintstone as a dentist the year
previous?

4. Councilmember George is a real estate broker. One of his
agents sells Mr. Hotel some property in a multi-family residential
zone which does not permit the construction of hotels. Several
months later, Mr. Hotel applies to have the property that was sold
to him by the real estate firm of Councilmember George rezoned for
hotel purposes. Is Councilmember George precluded from voting
because his brokerage received income from Mr. Hotel, even though
Councilmember George will not receive any financial benefit from
his decision concerning Mr. Hotel's property?

5. Councilmember Ida is the wife of an apartment house
owner. Mr. Jones was a tenant during 1977 paying a rent of $300
per month. Mr. Jones, who is the owner of a cab company, now
seeks an Owner's Permit from the City to operate his cabs in the
City. Would Mrs. Ida be prohibited from considering and voting
on Mr. Jones' permit because her spouse received income from
Mr. Jones during the past twelve months?

These are just a few of the many examples which indicate that if
Government Code Section 87103 were interpreted to prevent any
member from voting on any issue in which he has received income
in the past twelve months in excess of $250, such an interpre-
tation would not only appear to be absurd, but would not be
necessary to achieve the objectives of the Political Reform Act,
which we believe are to assure the independence, impartiality,
and honesty of public officials in governmental actions and
decisions and to assure that no public official will have eco-
nomic interests which are in substantial conflict with the proper
exercise of his official duties and powers.

We are of the opinion that our first interpretation would
support the validity of the Section while at the same time
achieving the objectives of the Act.

It is difficult to see how any involvement by Councilmember
Stone concerning the Industrial Area would have a material
effect on any income that he has received within twelve months
of making a decision since his decision will not in any way

-7-

Mr. Dwight Dickers
Fair Political Practices Commission
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

May 10, 1978
Page 8

change the amount of income that he will receive. But because
the Government Code is vague and unclear in this area and the
regulations adopted by the FPPC provide no assistance in the
interpretation of this Section, we request assistance in properly
advising Councilmember Stone and the City Council whether there
would be a conflict of interest if Councilmember Stone (1) participates in, or (2) votes on any of the above decisions.

We would very much appreciate your urgent consideration of this
request. As you can note, there are several immediate decisions
to be made by the City and since Councilmember Stone is the senior
member of the Council, the Council may desire to have his comments
and vote. It is important that the City have an independent
opinion because the opinion of this office, whatever it is, will
certainly be subject to the accusation that, since the City Attorney is appointed by the Council, he will be rendering a subservient opinion.

Thanking you very much for your cooperation and assistance in
this matter, we are

Very sincerely yours,

Jack Allen
JACK ALLEN
Acting City Attorney

JA:s

-8-

sections.facts:
The Industrial Area consists of an area of the City approximately ten blocks (40 acres) in size. It is presently, and has been for many years, zoned for certain industrial, commercial, and any residential use. The area consists of industrial and commercial uses as well as public utilities and municipal service facilities of the City of Beverly Hills. The City is a large landowner in the area, owning four large lots (6 acres), none of which are contiguous. Maple Properties has acquired eight lots (8.5 acres), approximately the same size as the City lots, comprising approximately two square blocks in the Industrial Area. Three of the properties owned by Maple Properties are separated either by City lots or by a street. It would be a substantial advantage to Maple Properties to have their properties consolidated, and it would be a substantial advantage to the City if it could consolidate its properties which are also divided by Maple Properties.

Since 1965, the City's General Plan has called for elimination of all the industrial uses in the Industrial Area and the creation of a substantial park area in the Industrial Area. In 1965, the plan called for the entire Industrial Area to become public property, but because of economics it never occurred. In 1973, the entire General Plan was under study for a revision, and the Citizens Advisory Committees recommended again that the industrial uses be eliminated from the Industrial Area and that a park of approximately eight acres be established with the remainder of the Industrial Area being re-zoned for multi-family residential uses.

On May 17, 1977, after three years of study by the Planning Commission and the Council, the Council adopted a revised Land Use Element which provided that the Industrial Area become a planned development area with a permitted density of 50 units per acre, a floor area ratio of 2.0:1 and a maximum height of 60 feet of any building. The Land Use Element also provided for parks to be located in the Industrial Area. The Revised Land Use Element was adopted by a 3 to 0 vote of the Council with Mayor Stone not participating.

The revised Land Use Element required that new sections of the zoning code be prepared to reflect the proposal for the Planned Development Area in the Industrial Area and to prepare a Specific Plan for the Planned Development Area. The Element provided that

Mr. Dwight Dickerson  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

Approximately one third of the RM-PD zone is owned by the Maple Properties, and it is the only owner in the area that could possibly take advantage of the planned development provisions.

POSSIBLE DECISIONS INVOLVING THE CITY COUNCIL

The following are potential decisions which the City Council will have to make involving the Industrial Area and which Councilmember Stone would or would not participate in depending upon whether he has a conflict of interest.

On May 16th, the City Council will hold hearings to amend the Land Use Element of the General Plan which reduces the densities permitted in the Planned Development area from 50 units to 25 units an acre, but establishes the height limits as described herein. The amendment also provides for the Limited Commercial area in the northeastern area of the Industrial Area. Maple Properties owns no interest in the Limited Commercial Area. The revisions also provide for the expanded open space areas for Multiple-Family Residential Development.

On June 6, 1978, the Council will hold public hearings on an ordinance which establishes the RM-PD zone, the LC zone, and also provides that developments constructed for the purpose of providing housing for the elderly and the handicapped shall be permitted to build up to 100 units per acre and sets forth standards for such housing. The RM-PD zone provides for housing for the elderly and the handicapped, but the provisions of this ordinance concerning housing for elderly and handicapped persons have city-wide application.

On June 6, the Council will also consider an ordinance establishing regulations for non-conforming uses throughout the City. However, it will have particular impact on the RM-PD zone because if the RM-PD zone is adopted, all private uses of property will be non-conforming and would have to be abated at some time after 20 years. It would affect Maple Properties only to the extent that if Maple Properties chose not to develop their properties as multiple family residential but to maintain them in their current status, those uses would have to be abated after 20 years unless a use is discontinued on any property for a period of six months, in which case the use would have to be abated immediately.

The Council is strongly considering at present expanding the park from six acres to a much larger park. It is considering possible acquisitions of property to the west of the existing City properties which it may decide on June 6. The Council is also considering the possible acquisition or condemnation of part or all of the Maple Properties. These decisions will involve the hiring of appraisers, the possible approval of the purchases of the properties or the possible approval of condemnation actions involving the acquisition of these properties.

Mr. Dwight Dickeron  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

May 10, 1978

The Council will have to decide this summer whether to negotiate the property exchange with Maple Properties and whether to acquire other properties by acquisition or condemnation which are presently planned for the park.

THE OPINION OF THIS OFFICE

It is the considered opinion of this office that if Councilmember Stone participates in any of the decisions facing the Council there would be no conflict of interest. The opinion of this office is predicated on the basis that Councilmember Stone does not own any property, has no investments in the Industrial Area, and he has no client which has an interest in the Industrial Area from which he is receiving income. Thus, Councilmember Stone would not have an "economic incentive for deciding a particular official matter without regard to its merits, or with regard to its effect upon the official's pocketbook". (County of Nevada v. MacMillen, 11 Cal.3d 662, 674 [1974]).

It has been said that the Political Reform Act of 1974 enforces the concept that a man may not serve two masters, particularly if one of the masters happens to be economic self-interest. (See U.S. v. Mississippi Valley Company, 364 U.S. 520 [1960]). Then Councilmember Stone would no longer be subjected to a second master, a master of economic self-interest.

We are aware of the provisions of Government Code Section 87100 which states:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in the making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a government decision which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."

There would be no problem because it would be obvious since on the facts stated the Councilmember has no present or future interest in any decision involving the Industrial Area or the Maple Properties which would benefit him. The problem is, however, with the definition of "financial interest" in Government Code Section 87103(c). The applicable part of the Section reads:

"87103. Financial Interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

"(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or . . . . "

This can be read two ways. The logical reading is that unless any decision made by Councilmember Stone will have a material financial effect on income he has received during the twelve months prior to making the decision, he has no conflict of interest. From the facts stated no such financial effect will occur.

The other interpretation, one that does not make sense, is that if Councilmember Stone has received income from Maple Properties in the last twelve months prior to the decision he cannot vote if the decision will have a material financial effect on his former client. To read the Section that if a councilmember, or for that matter, any affected official, is disqualified from voting because he received income within twelve months prior to making the decision, would appear contrary to the purposes of the Act and such an interpretation could render the Section constitutionally vague and ambiguous.

There are too many situations which arise in which such an application would not be rational. A few examples are set forth herein:

1. Councilmember Abel was a shoe salesman for Saks Fifth Avenue during 1977 and received an income in excess of $250. Councilmember Abel quit his job at Saks Fifth Avenue and went to work as an automobile salesman for Beverly Hills Cadillac. Saks Fifth Avenue applies for a building permit to double the size of their store and the permit is denied by the Building Director. Saks Fifth Avenue appeals the decision to the City Council. Must Councilmember Abel, who will not be financially affected in any way by the decision, but his decision will have a material financial effect on Saks Fifth Avenue, be prohibited from voting on the application?

2. Councilmember Baker is an independent contractor who sells encyclopedias. He sells a set of encyclopedia to Mr. Charlie from which he deducts a commission of $300 during 1977. Later, Mr. Charlie applies to the City Council for a permit to operate a private club. Is Councilmember Baker precluded from voting on Mr. Charlie's application for a private club merely because he received income in excess of $250 from Mr. Charlie in the previous 12 months?

3. Dr. Dentist is a Councilmember who provides dental services for the Flintstone family and has been paid more than $500 in dental fees by Mr. Flintstone during the past twelve months. Mr. Flintstone asks the City Council to relieve him of the requirement that he be required to provide 50 parking spaces on his building site and that he be permitted to provide those 50 spaces within 500 feet of his building. When Dr. Dentist learns that Mr. Flintstone is going to make such an application to the City, he tells Mr. Flintstone that he can no longer be the Flintstone family dentist. Is Dr. Dentist precluded from voting as a Councilmember on Mr. Flintstone's application because he received income from Mr. Flintstone as a dentist the year previous?

4. Councilmember George is a real estate broker. One of his agents sells Mr. Hotel some property in a multi-family residential zone which does not permit the construction of hotels. Several months later, Mr. Hotel applies to have the property that was sold to him by the real estate firm of Councilmember George rezoned for hotel purposes. Is Councilmember George precluded from voting because his brokerage received income from Mr. Hotel, even though Councilmember George will not receive any financial benefit from his decision concerning Mr. Hotel's property?

5. Councilmember Ida is the wife of an apartment house owner. Mr. Jones was a tenant during 1977 paying a rent of $300 per month. Mr. Jones, who is the owner of a cab company, now seeks an Owner's Permit from the City to operate his cabs in the City. Would Mrs. Ida be prohibited from considering and voting on Mr. Jones' permit because her spouse received income from Mr. Jones during the past twelve months?

These are just a few of the many examples which indicate that if Government Code Section 87103 were interpreted to prevent any member from voting on any issue in which he has received income in the past twelve months in excess of $250, such an interpretation would not only appear to be absurd, but would not be necessary to achieve the objectives of the Political Reform Act, which we believe are to assure the independence, impartiality, and honesty of public officials in governmental actions and decisions and to assure that no public official will have economic interests which are in substantial conflict with the proper exercise of his official duties and powers.

We are of the opinion that our first interpretation would support the validity of the Section while at the same time achieving the objectives of the Act.

It is difficult to see how any involvement by Councilmember Stone concerning the Industrial Area would have a material effect on any income that he has received within twelve months of making a decision since his decision will not in any way

Mr. Dwight Dickerson  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
Re: Possible Conflict of Interest - Stone

change the amount of income that he will receive. But because the Government Code is vague and unclear in this area and the regulations adopted by the FPPC provide no assistance in the interpretation of this Section, we request assistance in properly advising Councilmember Stone and the City Council whether there would be a conflict of interest if Councilmember Stone (1) participates in, or (2) votes on any of the above decisions.

We would very much appreciate your urgent consideration of this request. As you can note, there are several immediate decisions to be made by the City and since Councilmember Stone is the senior member of the Council, the Council may desire to have his comments and vote. It is important that the City have an independent opinion because the opinion of this office, whatever it is, will certainly be subject to the accusation that, since the City Attorney is appointed by the Council, he will be rendering a subservient opinion.

Thanking you very much for your cooperation and assistance in this matter, we are

Very sincerely yours,

[Signature]
JACK ALLEN
Acting City Attorney

JA:s

embedding.qa_text: June 9, 1978 Mr. Jack Allen Acting City Attorney 450 North Crescent Drive Beverly Hills, California 90210 Dear Mr. Allen: Thank you for your letter of May 10, 1978, on behalf of Councilmember Richard A. Stone, concerning the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act. We hope that the following advice may be helpful. The facts as we understand them are as follows. Councilmember Stone, an attorney, represented the partnership known as Maple Properties until that relationship was severed in January of 1978. During the course of his representation, Mr. Stone gave advice to Maple Properties concerning the acquisition, financing, development, and other legal aspects of obtaining real property owned by Litton Industries in the industrial area of Beverly Hills. When it became apparent that representing Maple Properties might interfere with his duties as a public official, Councilmember Stone refrained from participating or voting on any matters affecting the industrial area. In January 1978, Councilmember Stone severed his relationship with Maple Properties. Although he has received income in excess of $250 from Maple Properties for services rendered through December of 1977, he is not now receiving any income from Maple Properties, nor does he have any other financial interest in the city's industrial area. The industrial area is approximately ten blocks (40 acres) in size. For many years, the area has been zoned for certain industrial, commercial, and any residential uses. Currently, the area contains industrial and commercial uses as well as City of Beverly Hills public utilities and municipal service facilities. The city is a large landowner in the area, owning four large lots (6 acres), none of which are contiguous. Maple Properties has acquired eight lots (8.5 acres), approximately the same size as the city lots, comprising approximately two square blocks in the industrial area. Three of the properties owned by Maple Properties are separated either by city lot or by a street. It would be a substantial advantage to Maple Properties to have their properties consolidated, and it would be a substantial advantage to the city if it could consolidate its properties which are also divided by Maple's lots. There is currently a moratorium on new building in the industrial area. This moratorium is expected to expire at the end of the year. The planning commission has now proposed zoning for the industrial area which would provide that two blocks on the northeastern side of the industrial area be zoned for limited commercial (LC) and that the remainder of the industrial area would be zoned for multiple-family residential-planned development (RM-PD). Approximately one-third of the RM-PD zone is owned by the Maple Properties, and it is the only owner in the area that could possibly take advantage of the planned development provisions. There are presently several matters before the Council which involve the industrial zone. In June of 1978, the Council will hold public hearings on an ordinance which establishes the RM-PD zone, the LC zone, and also provides that developments constructed for the purposes of providing houses
